data.title: Politics And The Markets 07/04/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: CoreWeave: I Locked In 170% 3-Month Profits To Hunt Elsewhere (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) analysis, the stock has gained 90% in price.

I bought CoreWeave stock on April 2nd, 2025, just after its IPO on March 28, 2025. I sold out my position on July 2, 2025, for a 170% three-month return, because I consider the stock's elite return window to be fully closed now. I still expect the stock to gain in valuation from here, but much more moderately (i.e., 15% or so in 12 months in a bull case).

Fortune Favors the Bold, and Those with Restraint

I didn't buy CoreWeave knowing it would deliver 170% in three months. In my first CoreWeave analysis on March 31, 2025, I outlined a base-case price target of $100 in 12 months and $260 in a bull case. We're currently at $153 at the time of this writing, and while I feel confident that upside will continue from here, returns are becoming more speculative. I think the current valuation is above fair value. Let's look at this in more detail together.

The company's business model is based on debt rather than equity raising to finance its growth, which helps investors in the near term from an equity dilution point of view. That said, stock-based compensation ("SBC") is still very high. In the last trailing 12 months ("TTM"), $207.3 million has been spent on SBC, so investors are not completely free of dilution risk. Management could also issue equity outside of SBC for acquisitions or other reasons.

On the balance sheet, total common shares outstanding are currently 435.5 million. I conservatively forecast that this increases to 455 million in 12 months. After all, management may buy back shares or management may issue equity even though the business plan is to rely on debt. A moderate increase in my forecast reduces return estimation risk.

If I also forecast trailing 12-month revenue of about $9 billion in 12 months, the company will have a market cap of $90 billion if its price-to-sales ("P/S") ratio contracts from the current 13 to 10 due to moderating growth. With 455 million shares, that puts the stock price at close to $200. As the current stock price is $150, that indicates a 33.3% return on the surface. As I've already locked in 170% gains in three months, I'm not inclined to take the large risks that are involved with this investment for another 33.3% in four times my holding period. The downside risks of this investment are as spectacular as the upside is and has been. That's not to say the stock won't keep trending up over time, but reality is about to set in. That means no more 170% three-month returns—that window may be gone.

Bull-Case Predictions for CoreWeave in 12 Months
TTM Revenue $9 billion
P/S ratio 10
Market Cap Estimate $90 billion
Shares Outstanding 455 million
Stock Price Target $200
Implied Upside from the Current $150 Price 33.3%

Elite Investors Manage Risks and Don't Just Drive Returns

If we're looking at the downside risks here, TTM revenue in July 2026 could easily be $7.5 billion, and shares outstanding could rise to 500 million or more. At a P/S ratio of 9, the company's market cap would be $67.5 billion. Based on this model, the stock price would be $135. That's a 10% loss over 12 months, and that's not a loss I'm willing to risk. This is my base-case estimate, and while it is very conservative, I believe a high-risk investment like this deserves such a security protocol to protect returns.

Base-Case Predictions for CoreWeave in 12 Months
TTM Revenue $7.5 billion
P/S ratio 9
Market Cap Estimate $67.5 billion
Shares Outstanding 500 million
Stock Price Target $135
Implied Downside from the Current $150 Price 10%

In a bear case, we could see the AI sector lose sentiment early, but I find this very unlikely. I don't feel inclined to model a bear case because I consider it so unlikely that it becomes almost speculative. Instead, I'd prefer to use the midpoint of -10% and 33.3%—11.65%—as my official 12-month return projection. Given this outlook, you can see why my rating is a Hold. It's time to take your profits if you haven't already and cycle that cash into undervalued or stalwart high-alpha stocks.

The main downside risk operationally is that CoreWeave's existing backlog of over $25 billion is concentrated at about 80% in just two customers: Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI. Any loss or reduction in usage by Microsoft or OpenAI could severely impact revenue very quickly. But this is somewhat unlikely given current AI demand and CoreWeave's market-leading entrenched position.

Also, the elephant in the room is the company's total debt of $11.9 billion, with cash of just $1.28 billion. Total common equity is currently only $1.91 billion compared to $21.86 billion in total assets—so yes, the debt problem is real. However, management decides to deal with this later remains to be seen, but equity issuance will probably be a part of the strategy. The main issue is that CoreWeave is not selling bespoke designs that it can build a moat around and once at scale charge a premium. Instead, the data centers it builds are the moat—so this is a long-term play on infrastructure, and the good news is AI is not going away. But CoreWeave is essentially locked into a graphics processing unit demand cycle and must remain a leading provider over the long term for the company not to run into financial trouble. I believe it will succeed, and that its current valuation is somewhat fair, but I'm stepping away from this table for now as elite returns are better found elsewhere at this point.

Conclusion: Hold

In investing, just like in life, to be great you need two elements. The first is to know when to strike. The second is to know when to pull back. It's only when that art of balance is mastered that you can truly outperform the pack long term. It's no use buying at the ideal moment if you sell once the returns have dissolved. You need to buy at the ideal moment and lock in profits when returns are in their prime. With CoreWeave, I think this is the prime. Indeed, it happened in just three months, but from here on out, returns are going to normalize. No more 170% three-month gains—that window may be gone.

data.title: NYLI Balanced Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The first quarter saw a flurry of activity from the new administration, with dramatic policy changes implemented quickly, often chaotically. Aggressive tariff announcements were abruptly suspended, essential personnel were fired and rehired, and program terminations were not infrequently overturned by the courts, creating heightened uncertainty among investors, businesses, and consumers alike. Whereas “transitory” was the catchword in 2022, so “uncertainty” has come to dominate financial commentary in 2025. Though economic damage has thus far been minor, ongoing lack of policy clarity threatens future activity as corporations and households adopt a cautious stance.

Furthermore, the administration's unconventional economic policies have led to significant geopolitical shifts. Hostility toward allies like Canada and the

European Union (E.U.), combined with imperialistic ambitions, have stirred global concerns. The administration's intention to "get" Greenland is an example of growing cross-border tensions, adding to a more generalized anxiety.

Domestically, fears of higher inflation and slower growth, coupled with a lack of policy visibility, have fed into falling equity prices, with non-U.S. markets showing strong outperformance. This divergence in market returns underscores the climate of uncertainty and growing preference for overseas investments. In what is likely a safe haven bid, Treasury yields declined. As the new quarter unfolds, the administration must establish a consistent and transparent policy framework to calm domestic markets and maintain global economic relationships. Aggressive stances on economic and geopolitical fronts need careful management to avoid long-term repercussions. The current trajectory could lead to prolonged uncertainty and reduced economic activity, hindering growth in crucial sectors.

BENCHMARK 1

Russell 1000 Value Index

MORNINGSTAR CATEGORY

Moderate Allocation

FUND INFORMATION (As of 3/31/25)

4 No initial sales charge applies on investments of

$250,000 or more (and certain other qualified purchases). However, a contingent deferred sales charge of 1.00% may be imposed on certain redemptions made within 18 months of the date of purchase on shares that were purchased without an initial sales charge.

Portfolio Highlights

The NYLI Balanced Fund (the “Fund”) outperformed its benchmark only marginally this quarter but nevertheless managed to land solidly amongst the top quartile of funds within Morningstar’s Moderate Allocation category, capping off strong year over year performance. While some positive excess return was generated through active management, it was the structural value orientation of the fund that provided the greatest lift. After several years of leadership, growth stocks are arguably richly valued, and the bloom may finally be coming off the rose as growth expectations are declining and foreign tech competition heats up.

What Helped:

As noted above, outperformance versus peers was driven primarily by the Fund’s focus on value stocks, whereas many competitors offer more of a blend of value and growth. Fears of slowing growth – or even a possible recession – coupled with questions about Artificial Intelligence (AI) investments in the wake of the release of the DeepSeek models really seemed to take the wind out of the sails of high-flying growth stocks. But even relative to a value benchmark, the equity sleeve of the Fund enjoyed a strong quarter. This was a function of security selection, although that manifests in part by way of sector allocations which arise as a by- product of stock picking. Being overweight both healthcare and energy was helpful as was being underweight industrials. BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY), an off- benchmark British defense contractor, was the largest single contributor to performance, having popped following news that Germany was lifting its debt brake for defense outlays and was preparing a massive armaments budget. AIG, Elevance Health (ELV), Gilead Sciences (GILD), and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY)(OTCQX:RHHBF)(OTCPK:RHHVF)wrapped up the top five contributors. On the fixed income side, curve positioning helped at the margin. Allocations to asset backed securities also added incrementally to return.

What Hurt:

Just as the Fund benefited from being centered on value stocks, so too did its structural focus on U.S. securities weigh on results. After years of leadership, the baton was passed from the U.S. to overseas markets. Factors that may have contributed to that rotation include impressive technological achievements by Chinese players; European plans for massive fiscal expansion; and a U.S.-against-the-rest-of-the-world approach to trade policy. Weighing more modestly on performance was an emphasis on corporate credit within the bond portfolio with utility and industrial borrowers being particularly weak. Beta policy also detracted slightly as stocks were favored over bonds for most of a period in which bonds narrowly edged out the Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) used express that equity overweight. Within equities, the election not to hold Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) proved the largest detractor after shares the conglomerate rose following its highest ever quarterly profit and improvement in insurance operations. Active weights in Sempra (SRE), Gentex (GNTX), KKR & Co (KKR) and Philip Morris (PM) were also material detractors.

Outlook and Positioning Drafting this note in the immediate wake of the "Liberation Day" tariff announcements, we find ourselves at a critical juncture. The recent tariff impositions have sent ripples through the global economic landscape, creating a heightened sense of unease among investors and businesses alike. Sentiment, which had already waned considerably in Q1, is likely to fall further, constraining business capital expenditures and suppressing household consumption moving forward. These developments are poised to have a profound impact on the economic activities in the months ahead. While incoming data has thus far been relatively stable, it is important to recognize that this stability may be partly attributable to tariff frontrunning. Businesses have likely accelerated their operations and households their larger purchases to preempt the anticipated trade restrictions. As a result, stale data may not accurately reflect today’s underlying economic conditions. These concerns over trade policy will remain front and center for the foreseeable future, as it becomes increasingly clear that the administration is determined to reshuffle global trade flows via whatever means necessary. This aggressive stance is likely to come as quite a shock, dismantling a global economic framework that has been meticulously built over generations and from which we have benefited enormously. The probability of a recession is rising rapidly. Stock prices have already pared prior gains, yet price multiples remain elevated relative to historical norms, implying there is plenty of downside potential. The same holds true for credit markets, where growing vulnerabilities may bring widening credit spreads and increasing default rates. We view the risk/reward profile of equities as being generally unfavorable, and yet we are reluctant to be underweight as doing so is a dangerous game – I is difficult to identify turning points, and as we all recognize, stocks rise more often than they fall. That said, we did dial back equity exposure coming into the quarter such that we are near neutral or slightly underweight. We are content to hover here for now and look for an opportunity to aggressively add risk at some point in the quarters ahead once the dust around tariffs has settled.

Returns represent past performance which is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate, and shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance reflects transfer agency expenses charged to each share classes which will be limited to a maximum of 0.35% of that share class’s average daily net assets on an annual basis (excluding small account fees) after deducting any other applicable expense cap reimbursements or transfer agency waivers. This agreement will remain in effect until 2/28/26, and renew automatically for one-year terms unless written notice is provided prior to the start of the next term or upon approval of the Board. Visit newyorklifeinvestments.com for the most recent month-end performance. Expenses stated are as of the fund’s most recent prospectus.

† Effective 8/28/24, MainStay Balanced Fund was renamed NYLI Balanced Fund.

Before You Invest

Before considering an investment in the Fund, you should understand that you could lose money.

The investment strategies, practices and risk analyses used by the Subadvisor may not produce the desired results. The principal risk of investing in value stocks is that the price of the security may not approach its anticipated value. Investing in mid-cap stocks may carry more risk than investing in stocks of larger, more well-established companies. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. These risks may be greater for emerging markets. The Fund may invest in derivatives, which may increase the volatility of the Fund’s NAV. The principal risk of mortgage- related and asset-backed securities is that the underlying debt may be prepaid ahead of schedule, if interest rates fall, thereby reducing the value of the fund’s investment. If interest rates rise, less of the debt may be prepaid. Funds that invest in bonds are subject to interest-rate risk and can lose principal value when interest rates rise. Bonds are also subject to credit risk, in which the bond issuer may fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner. The Fund may experience a portfolio turnover rate of over 100% and may generate short-term capital gains which are taxable.

1. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000® Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. The Balanced Composite Index consists of the Russell 1000® Value Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Government/Credit Bond Index weighted 60%/40%, respectively. The Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Government/Credit Bond Index measures the performance of U.S. dollar denominated U.S. treasuries, government-related and investment-grade U.S. corporate securities that have a remaining maturity of greater than one year and less than ten years.

Index results assume the reinvestment of all capital gain and dividend distributions. An investment cannot be made directly into an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index is generally representative of the market sectors or types of investments in which the Fund invests. 2. The Annual Turnover Rate measures how quickly securities in the Fund are either bought or sold during the 12 months as of the most recent annual shareholder report. 3. Average annual total returns include the change in share price and reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. Class I shares are generally only available to corporate and institutional investors.

Consider the Funds' investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. The prospectus and summary prospectus include this and other information about the Funds and are available by visiting the Prospectus. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

New York Life Investment Management LLC serves as the Fund's Manager. NYL Investors LLC, is a wholly owned subsidiary of New York Life Insurance Company and Wellington Management Company LLP is unaffiliated to New York Life Investment Management.

“New York Life Investments” is both a service mark, and the common trade name, of certain investment advisors affiliated with New York Life Insurance Company. Securities distributed by NYLIFE Distributors LLC, 30 Hudson Street, Jersey City, NJ 07302, Member FINRA/SIPC.

NOT FDIC INSURED | MAY LOSE VALUE | NO BANK GUARANTEE

data.title: Primerica: Low-Risk Business Model Makes Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of Primerica (NYSE:PRI) have been a solid performer over the past year, though the stock has yet to fully recapture its pre-Liberation Day level even as the market has surged to all-time highs. Within the insurance sector, Primerica is a unique player, given less sensitivity to interest income or mortality, instead focused on generating recurring fee income. While this does lead to a premium multiple relative to peers, I believe much of this premium is justified by the company’s business mix.

Primerica is, overall, a niche player in the broader insurance industry, though it has a significant presence in the space it operates. The company is the 3rd largest issuer of term life policies, with over $950 billion of in-force policies. Because the vast majority of term policies expire before the policyholder dies, their premiums are relatively inexpensive, especially in comparison to a whole-life policy.

While whole-life policies also can serve as an estate planning or retirement vehicle, term-life policies are primarily intended to cover the policyholder’s working years so that if they were to pass away, their family could make up the lost income. This is why Primerica caters specifically to a middle-class audience. The average household income of its clients is about $83,000. The average term life policy is $255,000. Beyond term life policies, Primerica does sell investments, and their average mutual fund investment is about $12,000. It does not manage these investments, instead acting as a distributor for other funds. The company has over 150,000 licensed salespeople, giving it significant distribution capabilities.

As you can see below, insurance premiums on its term life policies represent the majority of the business. Next, its investment services business generates substantial fee-based revenue. These revenues are tied to commissions upon the sale of a product. Additionally, Primerica earns recurring asset-based (or “sponsor”) revenue, whereby the mutual fund manager pays a piece of the management fee back to Primerica. This revenue stream will have sensitivity to the market, given it is tied to AUM levels.

Investment income is then a very small piece of the business, representing about 5% of revenue and about 20% of net income. That is because term life policies do not have the same asset/liability matching dynamics as annuities or whole life policies. Additionally, one key distinguishing factor is that even in its insurance operation, Primerica functions more like a distributor. That is because it reinsures 80-90% of the mortality risk, under a yearly reinsurance contract. As a result, it is more exposed to persistency of policies, rather than mis-modelling mortality. This reduces the capital and asset-intensity of the business, which is why it has just $4.5 billion in investment assets despite the large headline $950 billion of policies in force.

Importantly, recent results have been solid. In the company’s first quarter reported on May 7th, Primerica earned $5.02, which beat estimates by $0.23 as revenue grew by 9%. Term Life revenue rose by 4% to $458 million, with operating income up 6% to $147 million. Its benefits ratio was 58.2%, similar to last year’s 58%. The lapse rate remains elevated, though recent policies are performing in-line with expectations.

Given its focus on middle-income consumers, the company has seen some pressure in sales growth from tighter budgets after the multiyear inflation wave. This has made some consumer less willing to spend on a term-life policy. It wrote 86,000 policies, which was flat to last year. Monthly sales per representative ticked down to 0.19 from 0.20 given this pressure. Historically, monthly production has been in the 0.20-0.24 range, so demand is running at depressed levels.

Given the uncertainty around tariffs, I expect to see sales activity remaining modest in coming quarters, though over time, there should be increased activity as sales return to more normalized levels. I am looking for premium growth of 3-5% this year, given the benefit of 2024 sales and a growing sales force, which can mitigate slower sales per representative.

While term-life sales were slow, investment sales activity was strong. Investment and savings products saw sales revenue grow by 25% to $111 million as there were $3.6 billion of investment sales, up 28% from last year. Meanwhile, asset-based revenue jumped 18% to $152 million, leading to overall revenue growth of 19%. Client assets grew by 6% to $110 billion, as net client flows were $800 million.

Asset-based revenue has been particularly strong as product mix and sponsor contracts have supported improved economics for Primerica. The company earned 5.3bps of asset-based revenue per dollar of client assets from 5.1bps last year. Over time, this revenue stream should largely track AUM, which is primarily driven by the market. Given the April sell-off before the recovery, I expect Q2 revenue to be flattish sequentially, but with the market now hitting all-time highs, we should see a solid Q3. This unit also enjoys strong operating leverage, so operating income jumped 24% to $81 million.

Finally, while relatively small, net investment income is still an important part of its earnings. Primerica had net investment income of $42 million in Q1, up from $38 million a year ago. This equates to just about $1 of EPS, or 20% of earnings. As noted earlier, it has a ~$4 billion portfolio, which is quite conservative. It is primarily focused in fixed income and has a 5-year duration at a 4.1% book yield. About 99% of the portfolio is investment grade, reducing credit risk. With its book yield below prevailing market yields, ongoing reinvestment should continue to support gradually higher investment income at least through year-end.

Finally, Primerica has a strong balance sheet with 21% debt to capital. One benefit of slowing term-life sales has been that the insurance operating company has built up more capital. It has a 470% risk-based capital ratio, which is extremely healthy. I like to see insurers operate with 400-425%, so Primerica has capital well in excess of this level, providing protection against a material downturn. Because of this, Primerica can return essentially all of its earnings to shareholders, as it already has excess capital which can support an eventual reacceleration of policy demand.

The company has been doing just that. It did $118 million of buybacks last quarter, and there has been about a 5% share count reduction over the past year. It also pays a 1.5% dividend yield. I would expect buybacks to continue at least at Q1’s pace, and if we see slower term policy sales, there would be scope to accelerate repurchase activity. Even with its excess capital position and slower sales, PRI is generating a substantial 30% return on equity, which is why shares can trade at such a premium to its $68 book value. Its excess capital position is about ~$7/share in my view.

Assuming the S&P 500 stays above 6,100, I see Primerica poised to earn $21.25-$22 this year, giving shares a ~12.8x multiple. As you can see below, shares are trading at a premium to other financials, though the company is trading at a slight discount to its own history. In my view, Primerica should trade at a premium to peers in this environment. Its revenue streams are more recurring in nature, with less actual underwriting risk, given its reinsurance contracts. Additionally, insurers with large investment portfolios may see some pressure on income in coming quarters if the Fed does, in fact, cut interest rates. This pressure is more modest for PRI.

With at least $20 of sustainable annual capital return capacity, PRI has a potential 7.2% capital return yield. Even with just ~3% underlying growth, given inflation and household growth for term life sales, and ongoing market growth over the long term for asset-based revenue, I believe PRI should sustainably grow at least that quickly. With a 7% starting yield and 3+% long-term growth, Primerica is positioned to deliver 10+% long-term returns at its current share price of $277. As such, I view it as an attractive long-term investment and rate shares a “buy.”

data.title: Employment Report: 147K Jobs Added In June, More Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 147,000 jobs were added in June, up from 144,000 in May and higher than the expected 111,000 addition. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate unexpectedly inched lower to 4.1%.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 147,000 in June, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.1 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Job gains occurred in state government and health care. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.1 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.0 million, changed little in June. The unemployment rate has remained in a narrow range of 4.0 percent to 4.2 percent since May 2024.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 147,000 in June, in line with the average monthly gain of 146,000 over the prior 12 months. In June, job gains occurred in state government and health care. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last four years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 150,000, up from the previous month.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 151,000, up from the previous month.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived, as irrational exuberance quickly took over.

The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.117% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.12%, slightly below the latest 12-month moving average of 4.15%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong. The most recent trough occurred 22 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but has since trended downward. It now stands at 1.0%, up from the previous month.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 23.0 weeks, up from the previous month. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks.

The U6 series currently stands at 7.7%, down from the previous month.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.3%, the lowest level since 2022.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.6%, down from the previous month.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.7%, the lowest level since January 2022.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: XSD: Relative Strength Emerging In SMID Semis, Reiterate Buy Amid The AI Boom
data.description:
With big moves lately from the likes of NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), and Micron (MU), it's easy to get lulled into thinking that large- and mega-cap semiconductor stocks are the only game in town. I was intrigued by the chart below from RenMac. They point out that small-cap tech and chip stocks are performing very well versus the large-cap I.T. sector index.

So, I'm revisiting the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (NYSEARCA:XSD). It's a modified equal-weight semiconductor equity ETF, holding US stocks. I was bullish on the fund back in October of last year. It has been a wild ride, but shares are up by more than 6% in the past nine months, performing about in line with the S&P 500. I reiterate a buy rating today.

Small Tech & Semis Outperforming

According to the issuer, XSD seeks to track the modified equal-weighted S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index, which provides the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large, mid, and small-cap stocks. It is a tactical fund that is used to express industry views on semi stocks within the Information Technology sector.

XSD is a small ETF with just $1.3 billion in assets under management as of July 2, 2025. That's down from $1.4 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month yield is about a percentage point below that of the S&P 500 at 0.29%.

Share-price momentum has been stellar, however, earning the product a strong A- ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system. The fund is highly risky, though, particularly given its recent annualized volatility readings, which were pushed upward by the post-Liberation Day selloff. The good news is that while it has a focused allocation to a single US industry, it's not all that concentrated.

Finally, liquidity is mixed, as evidenced by low average daily volume near 50,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of 11 basis points. So, I would still encourage prospective investors to use limit orders around the market's open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star ETF by Morningstar has a mixed allocation across the style box. It leans toward the growth style, with just 15% classified as value. What's more, there's a high position in domestic SMID caps-US large caps are only 27%. Thus, it will be more cyclical than, say, the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH), which has a higher weighting in mega-cap growth names.

As it stands, the price-to-earnings ratio is high at 29, but the long-term EPS growth rate is also sturdy, above 14%. The resulting PEG ratio is reasonable, around 2. And with solid relative technical and momentum strength off the April low, I believe a long position still makes sense today.

XSD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, it's all tech-pretty straightforward. Dig deeper, though, and we find that the top 10 holdings are some familiar ones, but also some lesser-known companies in the chip space. What I like is that the fund is not solely dependent on a few huge stocks to carry the load. Thus, the upcoming Q2 reporting season will be key price action as the second half unfolds.

XSD: Holdings & Dividend Information

Speaking of seasonality, we are in the throes of a strong month when analyzing the past 10 years of price data. May, July, and November are the top months, but I would warn investors that the August through early October stretch often sees bouts of volatility.

XSD: Bullish Mid-Year Trends

The Technical Take

With a premium valuation and relative strength emerging, XSD's technical situation is mixed, but healthy. Notice in the chart below that shares are once again encroaching on the all-time highs set last July and immediately in advance of the China DeepSeek AI announcement this past January. The $273 to $274 range is obvious resistance, and I see support down at the long-term 200-day moving average, currently near $233.

But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It's ranging in a bullish zone between 50 and 75. I also like that there's now a high amount of volume by price below the current level; that suggests that there should be a solid amount of buying support on a pullback. Finally, there remains a gap way down at $210 that would only get filled amid a steep selloff.

As for a long-term upside target, if we take the $116 depth of the 2024 to April 2025 drawdown and add that on top of the $273 resistance point, then an upside measured move price objective to near $390 would be in play upon a breakout.

XSD: Bullish Breakout Pending, Strong RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on XSD. I see relative strength taking shape among small- and mid-cap semiconductor stocks. While the P/E is by no means a bargain, its high growth could work well should the AI boom persist.

data.title: Stars, Stripes, And A $4 Trillion Tab
data.description:
Wall Street celebrated American independence early with record highs, trillions in new debt, and a job market just soft enough to keep hope alive. Stocks surged into the long weekend with all the subtlety of fireworks in a drought - and about the same regard for fiscal safety.

Level Change 7/3/25 (%)

+0.8 Dow

+1.0 Nasdaq

+1.0 Nasdaq 100

+0.8 S&P 500

+0.7 S&P 400

+0.6 S&P 600

The big news - fittingly, Big and Beautiful - was the House’s final blessing of President Trump’s budget behemoth. After weeks of horse trading and procedural stunts (including Minority Leader Jeffries’ “Magic Minute” delay), the bill is now en route to the Oval, where it will no doubt be signed with a pen large enough to show up on satellite.

Estimates now put the bill’s cost at $4T over the next decade, a tidy upgrade from the modest $3.3T once assumed.

Maya MacGuineas of the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget called it “the single most expensive, dishonest, and reckless budget reconciliation bill ever,” a quote which may soon be featured on commemorative July Fourth mugs.

But never mind the tab. The market saw an uncertainty made certain, and it saluted. Big, beautiful debt-ceilings-as-decor, here we come.

On the economic front, June payrolls came in hot enough to barbecue a rate cut.

Payrolls rose by 147K, topping the 118K consensus. Private payrolls, the soft spot, rose just 74K, but upward revisions to prior months kept investors smiling. Unemployment ticked down to 4.1%, a surprise, until you notice it was less thanks to jobs than to vanishing jobseekers. Call it full employment by subtraction.

Wages grew just 0.2% in June, with the annual pace down to 3.7%. That’s slow enough to comfort rate-cutters, not slow enough to convince them. Jobless claims came in below forecasts, and continuing claims didn’t move.

In all, strong enough to delay a July cut, weak enough to keep September on the table - though odds for that dropped from foregone conclusion to flip-a-coin territory.

Investors responded with gusto, driving the S&P and Nasdaq to new highs. The holiday-shortened week wrapped on an optimistic note: jobs up, wages easing pressure on inflation, and the Treasury about to get busy converting political ambition into sovereign debt.

Quite a week, capped by Wall Street toasting America with the bubbly confidence of someone else footing the bill. Future taxpayers: to your health.

Happy Independence Day. Kelly Letter subscribers, I’ll see you Sunday.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fortinet: SASE Subscription Fuels Margins
data.description:
Unified Platform Drives Subscription Mix Shift

We initiate coverage on Fortinet, Inc. (NASDAQ:FTNT) at Strong Buy with $148 PT. Fortinet provides integrated and automated cybersecurity across firewall, cloud, and endpoint. While consensus expects moderating firewall growth, we see an inflecting, margin-led mix shift into subscription run-rate fueled by unified platform architectures (think platformization of ServiceNow and Palo Alto with balance-sheet discipline and margin profile).

We forecast unified SASE and SecOps ARR compounding at a 24% CAGR through FY26E, yet most consensus models assume only mid-20% growth and flat share counts. In our model, that high-teens subscription acceleration drives incremental operating leverage worth ~$0.04 of EPS annually. On top of that, we layer in an annual repurchase of ~7mn shares (remaining authorization $1.6bn), which further boosts EPS by ~$0.02 per year. The combination of faster‐than‐modeled ARR growth and overlooked buyback tailwinds delivers a structural $0.06 per share of "hidden" earnings power that Street forecasts have yet to capture.

A 50× FY26E P/E is demonstrable double-digit earnings plus a margin of safety, given multiples remain meaningfully below history despite capital returns and $1.9 bn of FCF per year. While a capex pause or tariffs could create inflections, modeled upside is open and risk is quantified. Price to intrinsic value sets one of few asymmetric risk/reward in software as we enter what should be a multi-year product cycle.

AI Platform Growth Nexus

FTNT represents a rare inflection in AI platform expansion and can compound recurring revenues and competitive advantage for years. The market has yet to recognize that FTNT's secure networking franchise (>50% share of global firewall deployments) is rapidly becoming a distribution vector for deep integration of proprietary generative AI across the Security Fabric, a network-embedded platform with machine learning, GenAI models and automated response engines natively operating across >40 functions and all layers of the network, endpoint and cloud stacks to enable containment and remediation of threats in seconds (industry average today is days), while creating a scalable cost base (visible in expanding service GMs at 78.5% and overall co-level OM at 34% in the latest quarter).

Unified SASE ARR grew 26% y/y to $1.15bn, Security Ops ARR grew 30% year over year to $434mn and >90% of the sales force is now certified to monetize the AI-powered suite, driving >100% billing growth in upsell solutions such as FortiSASE and FortiAI. We believe platformization through AI and an underlying OS built for convergence creates a unique flywheel between customers and the rich telemetry on the Security Fabric, which can be used to improve ML and accelerate future upgrades. This flywheel underpins FTNT's DD+ guide for service revenue growth (even as hardware cyclicality subsides) and supports EPS growth well ahead of consensus through 2026. We view the AI expansion as the underappreciated driver of FTNT's shift from security appliance vendor to enterprise platform, with further margin leverage to come.

Recurring Revenue Shield

FTNT is entering a structurally advantaged era of margin resilience and recurring revenue durability that should enable it to outperform sector peers as security trends evolve. The service revenue mix, now the majority of the top line, grew 20% in 2024 and surpassed $4.8bn, catapulting its profit engine beyond hardware. The subscription backlog ramp reflects rigorous execution in unifying SASE, SecOps and managed services across more than 800,000 accounts globally, with Europe, the Middle East and Africa leading all regions in both absolute and relative terms.

In 2024, growth in security subscription and support services more than offset hardware volatility, driving gross profit up 18 percent and operating income up 45 percent as operating expense leverage compounded. Deferred revenue, a leading indicator of backlog and future recurring streams, stood at more than $5.7 billion entering the calendar year 2025 estimate, a near 20 percent year-over-year increase. Billings attributable to unified SASE and SecOps now represent 35 percent of the total, further reinforcing forward revenue durability.

We expect these metrics to deliver at least a 19 percent earnings per share compound annual growth rate through the fiscal year 2027 estimate, as high-margin recurring service revenue shields hardware cyclicality and the fund's investments in cloud security and artificial intelligence-powered expansion. The shift to a recurring software and subscription core provides a rare valuation anchor in cybersecurity, underwriting asymmetric upside even if macro volatility persists.

Rationale for street-Beating EPS

Our FY25E / FY26E EPS projections of $2.55 / $2.95 imply 12.8% / 15.7% y/y growth versus the consensus of $2.47 / $2.77. These above-street views reflect a faster mix shift toward high-margin subscription-led revenues as the firewall-refresh super-cycle takes hold in 2H25 and Unified SASE / AI-driven SecOps ARR expands at a 24% CAGR through FY26.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $6.76B 33 $7.60B 37
EBITDA $1.70B 33 $1.91B 37
Net Income $1.69B 33 $2.41B 37
EPS $2.47 26 $2.77 27
SG&A Expenses $2.87B 33 $3.23B 37

We also assume repurchases of ~7mn shares annually, delivering a $0.06 to annual diluted EPS tailwind that consensus overlooks. Infosec management has proven its precision forecasting, delivering 5-10% EPS upside to guidance and averaging 18% above street consensus in recent quarters. Management's margin guidance of 31.5-33.5% for FY25E provides a floor as software mix increases, against which we model 32.8-33.5%. Key downside risks include tariff expansion, enterprise spending delays and competitive pricing responses, which, we believe, are factored into our 70% confidence score and scenario analysis. Overall, our conviction in above-street EPS stems from embedded operating leverage, strong product cycles and the factors above.

Valuation

Our $148 price target is based on a 50.0 times fiscal year 2026 estimated price to earnings ratio on our above-consensus $2.95 earnings per share, offering 44 percent upside and a Strong Buy rating. Our target multiple captures Fortinet's persistent 15.7 percent earnings per share compound annual growth rate and accelerating subscription revenue growth, leading 34 percent adjusted operating margins and consistent double-digit earnings per share beats.

The multiple sits below the historical median price to earnings ratio of 54.3 times and well below the peer median price to earnings ratio of 132.0 times (20th percentile versus 77th percentile), balancing Fortinet's dominant firewall position, secure access service edge-led expansion, and recurring $1.9bn free cash flow in fiscal year 2024 with allowances for insider selling and macro uncertainty. Fortinet's periods of trading near our target multiple have historically generated 64 percent in the forward twelve months, suggesting strong risk/reward. Overall, our valuation considers fundamentals and a margin of safety, reflecting the firm's inflection into a subscription-led model and leaving ample room for multiple expansion should the next product cycle arrive.

Risks to Our Strong Buy Thesis

While we assign high confidence to above-consensus EPS momentum from a subscription-led transformation and commanding firewall franchise, the thesis/valuation framework faces material risks. Most notably, escalations in U.S.-China/APAC tariffs could weigh on hardware sourcing and gross margin more than modeled (95% of FTNT manufacturing is in Taiwan; prolonged trade frictions or sanctions, especially amid escalating geopolitical tensions, could skew hardware production costs, inhibit supply and threaten modeled margin resilience and cash generation).

FTNT's moat also hinges on continued innovation ahead of a fast-evolving product taxonomy; if platform velocity stalls, the premium for integrated SASE/AI-driven SecOps could shrink materially if competitors such as PANW, CRWD and security-focused startups leapfrog its unified approach, jeopardizing FTNT's modeled ARR growth and multiple expansion. Lastly, investors should watch for macro-driven IT spend pauses broadening and firewall-refresh cycles extending beyond 2H25, as both would delay the subscription mix shift and recurring revenue compounding central to our EPS/valuation thesis.

Conclusion

We see Fortinet as one of the few asymmetric risk/reward stories in cyber, rooted in visible inflection to high-margin, subscription-led ARR growth and disciplined net earnings execution. With accelerating unified SASE and AI expansion, a sticky firewall franchise, and a below-historical and peer group multiple, we see material upside well ahead of consensus. Bottom line: We believe it's time to lean into the product cycle/compounding Op Leverage story-investors should treat short-term macro/tariff volatility as opportunities in a durable, margin-protected multi-yr growth story.

data.title: JOF: The Wide Discount To NAV Looks Set To Reduce
data.description:
Introduction

The $300m-sized Japan Small Capitalization Fund (NYSE:JOF), backed by Nomura Asset Management, has proven to be a very rewarding CEF [close-end fund] for its investors in the first half of 2025. In a year when the US markets (as represented by SPY), have only generated total returns of less than 7%, and global markets (as represented by VT) have just about managed double-digit returns, JOF, which focuses on close to 100 Japanese small-cap stocks, has outperformed these pockets by 2.5-4x.

If you’re swayed by this impressive performance, and are tempted to pursue a position in the same, here are some of the major subplots to consider.

Japanese Industrials Look Relatively Healthy, But Tariff Risk Looms

If JOF is to continue to flourish, we suspect, the industrial component of its portfolio will have to be in a good place. We say this because, Japanese industrial stocks dominate JOF, accounting for one-fourth of the total portfolio.

All in all, we feel that Japanese industrials are staring at a mixed outlook. Let’s start with what’s good. Firstly, we’re encouraged to see that the forward-looking manufacturing PMI recently crossed the expansionary barrier (readings over 50), for the first time in 13 months! The headline PMI number was also backed by improvements in output and employment.

Business sentiment for small Japanese manufacturers also appears to be trending in positive territory for now, after months of negative readings.

However, the clouds of tariffs are something that could jeopardize the relatively sanguine status quo. The latest report suggest that Japan is no close to agreeing a deal, and has, in fact, angered Trump to such an extent that he is now prepared to impose tariffs of 30-35% on Japanese exports, well above the 24% tariff that was initially announced in early April. This would come as a hammer blow to Japan, as the US is its largest export market.

Wide NAV to Discount on JOF, Looks Set To Narrow

As you can see from the image below, one of the long-standing drawbacks of JOF is that it has been trading at a steep discount to NAV for much of the last 15–20 years.

What’s worse is that as time has progressed, that discount has only widened, with the discount for the most recent year (as of March 2025) working out to over 18.4%, which is roughly 250bps more than what’s been seen over a 5-year time frame.

Fortunately, the board of JOF haven’t been viewing it with rose-tinted glasses, and last month, stated their intention to resort to a couple of initiatives that could help ameliorate the wide discount differential.

Firstly, they are going to follow a “level distribution plan” that will see monthly distributions equating to 10% of the NAV (as of May 31, 2025) on an annualized basis. This is quite a marked step-up as last year, the annualized distribution rate on the NAV was only a little over 3%.

For the next three months, they’ve already announced impressive distributions, equating to $0.0887 per share. For context, historically JOF has only distributed annually, and last year’s distribution worked out to only $0.3118.

By pivoting to the monthly distribution calendar, JOF is hoping to stoke up more interest from an untapped bracket of investors who normally would only stick to products with monthly distribution characteristics. With additional interest from this new cohort, there’s hope that the NAV discount could narrow.

In addition to the Level Distribution Plan, JOF could also launch a tender offer to buyback 10% of the outstanding shares, so long as the average daily discount to NAV, is wider than 9% from July 1st 2025 to March 31st 2026. As noted earlier, over the past year, the discount to NAV has averaged around 18%, so clearly there’s a lot of elbow room that they will have.

JOF’s Valuations Are Very Cheap

Besides these favorable developments, JOF is also likely to appeal to value conscious investors as its holdings are very cheaply priced, and the earnings runway that they offer are also not too dissimilar to what the holdings from the large-cap oriented abrdn Japan Equity Fund (JEQ) offer.

As per Morningstar data, JOF’s holdings can be picked up at a forward P/E of less than 8x, which works out to a 40% discount over JEQ. Yet, JEQ’s holdings are poised to deliver superior long-term earnings growth of only around 2%.

As an aside, investors may also want to be aware that even though JOF looks like it only pursues small-caps on paper (and this is also reflected in the average market-cap of its holdings that equates to $730m), the reality is that it also does offer some ownership to mid-caps which account for around a third of the total portfolio.

Closing Thoughts - Good Momentum, But Close To A Key Pivot Point

The fact that JOF is in the midst of bullish conditions can be gleaned from its daily chart, where the CEF is now trading well above its three key moving averages of 50, 100 and 200.

However before investors get carried away and join the bandwagon, they may also want to take note of developments on the larger time frame weekly chart.

What we can see is that after breaking out from an ascending channel (marked by the two dotted red lines), JEF has now reached a zone which has previously served as a pivot area. This could prompt a slowdown in bullish momentum, or potentially even a reversal in the price. Also note that the RSI indicator in the bottom half of the chart points to overbought conditions. The last couple of instances, when the RSI was at this juncture, CEF witnessed a price contraction.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Signs Of Activity Rebound
data.description:
Investment overview

NXP's (NASDAQ:NXPI) the largest vertical is the automotive segment, where it sells microcontrollers, software solutions for internal connectivity, and radars. NXP dominates the 77GHz radar transceiver market, with key applications in autonomous driving, alongside Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY). With the competition increasing between Google (GOOG)'s Waymo and Tesla (TSLA) in the US for the robotaxi race, NXP could be ideally positioned to benefit from such growth trends. Also, Chinese car manufacturers (led by BYD) are aggressively investing in ADAS features.

On top of that, it has a small exposure to EV via its solid positioning in battery management systems (where it competes with Analog Devices (ADI) and Texas Instruments (TXN). However, it has no exposure to power semiconductors.

The second segment is related to Industrial applications, where NXP owns a comprehensive portfolio of solutions for buildings, factories, trains, and tractors. In factories, semiconductors and software are enabling advanced automation and are a key to site productivity.

Then, the mobile unit handles solutions for device security (NFC wallet) and holds the largest market share, surpassing Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Growth could stem from the need for more security among connected devices. Finally, the firm operates in the communication infrastructure sector and dominates in High-Performance Radio Frequency power amplifiers used for 5G base stations. Unfortunately, the 5G industry CAPEX has disappointed over the recent years. It is unknown when this segment will bottom out.

NXP aims to grow its revenues by a CAGR of 8% in the coming years, driven by one quarter of its sales advancing at double-digit rates, thanks to positive penetration of advanced autonomous features within cars and AI applications. The legacy and core business (mobile, mobile infrastructure, and electronics non-correlated to ADAS in cars) are, however, expected to grow at a slow pace (3%) and underperform the global semiconductor market.

NXP holds solid market shares in microcontrollers MCUs and sensors. Overall, it is the second-biggest chip provider for the automotive industry.

In the next section, we will discuss recent quarterly results and highlight some notable advancements mentioned during the conference calls.

Recent quarterly trends

The management has recently stated, during the Q1 FY25 results, that forward revenues are expected to grow slightly sequentially. This is good news, as it stops the decline pattern seen in the graph below.

In addition, bullish comments indicated that we could be facing an activity rebound: “we are seeing some positive trends. These trends include improving distribution customer backlog levels as well as stabilized order signals from our direct customers. Additionally, we are experiencing an increase in short cycle orders as well as some spot product shortages leading to customer escalations. Taken together, these trends have historically been indicative of the early innings of improving cycle dynamics. »

Other peers, such as ON Semiconductor, have also recently indicated signs of activity rebound.

During the 53rd JPMorgan Tech conference call, the CEO transition was well explained. The current CEO Kurt Sievers insisted that the decision to leave was their own, and that they would go into early retirement after having well served the company. Kurt Sievers also introduced Rafael Sotomayor, who will take the CEO job by October 2025. He began his career at Intel, followed by a stint at Broadcom, where he developed a franchise in connectivity, then joined NXP in 2014 and managed the industrial and mobile business (close to half of the company in terms of revenues). Additionally, he co-developed the overall strategy, ensuring that the 2030 goal of doubling EPS through growth rebound and expanding the gross margin to 60% remains in place.

Also, the CEO reiterated its calls for a potential revenue bottom: « We think it's a sign of a cycle recovery », seeing better trends in terms of channel inventory and the level of customer backlogs. Another interesting part was related to the importance of China in NXP operations : « they are faster and they have to lead now in the transformation into SDVs. So, software defined vehicles, EVs, but also in industrial electronics, when it's about automation and robotics in factories, China is leading in the world. So, if we have now a priority call, where would we spend $1 R&D for a Western automotive company or for a Chinese one, we spend it for China. So that's the strategic ».

Despite China's willingness to increase its market share in the semiconductor market, most advanced electronics are likely to continue being produced by Western firms, including NXP. It is open to establishing both R&D and manufacturing sites in mainland China to serve its customers better, and notes that revenue directly coming from the Chinese market represents approximately 18% of its total sales.

Finally, NXP has recently been quite active in terms of M&A activity, having acquired three firms (Aviva, TTTech, and Kinara) for $1.1 billion to reinforce its high-tech capabilities, empowering its Software-Defined Vehicle CoreRide platform.

Let's examine the company's financials. Q1 FY25 Free Cash Flow was $427m, at a quarterly record low since 2024, but this could eventually be recognized as an activity bottom, given management commentaries. On the balance sheet, NXP recorded $4 billion in gross cash, $1.5 billion in short-term debt, and $10 billion in longer-term debt, well spread across maturities, as shown below. ND/EBITDA stood at 1.6X, which is reasonable and should allow buybacks and dividend payments to continue going forward, reaching a cumulative $2.4 billion in FY24 (4% of the market capitalization).

What valuation can we expect?

The main hypotheses used are the following:

  • Revenues are expected to rebound gradually starting in FY26, as distribution channels normalize, allowing under-shipping to end and growth to resume in the medium term, with an estimated 8% CAGR in line with management's end-market projections.
  • Gross margin expansion toward 60%, in line with management expectations (higher factory utilization, better product mix).
  • A FCF/revenues (cash-conversion) reaching 30% by the end of the decade, like most of the top-tier semiconductor companies.
  • By 2030, doubling Free Cash Flows, as the management sees X2 on EPS.

Using a WACC of 9% and a long-term growth rate of +3%, I calculate a DCF target price of USD 255 per share, indicating further upside potential

Risks

NXP faces several risk factors. First, competition is intense in the semiconductor industry, with several companies competing, including ON Semiconductor (ON), Infineon, STMicroelectronics (STM), Renesas (OTCPK:RNECF), Microchip (MCHP), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Analog Devices (ADI). Fortunately, we have seen that NXP holds solid market shares in its end markets. Continued investment and innovation are therefore key to maintaining its leadership. On top of that, Chinese semiconductor firms are becoming more competitive in the field of microcontrollers as they aim to reduce their dependence on Western companies. New entrants to the market could add pricing pressure to this mature market.

Then, cyclical risks could cause the short-term Price Earnings ratio to compress during a phase of economic slowdown or trade friction. We can see the impact of such events in the graph below.

Finally, a significant part of NXP's valuation stems from the growth expectations of its ADAS portfolio. NXP must deliver on that side or face low single-digit topline growth, which would negatively affect its valuation multiple.

Conclusion

NXP is ideally positioned in the semiconductor sector to serve the fast-growing ADAS segment, a key enabler to autonomous driving. The recent negative economic cycle and fears about trade tariffs have weakened financials and technicals. Good news could, however, be on the horizon as management indicates signs of activity rebound and inventory channel normalization going forward. With still upside potential, as noted in a DCF model, I rate the stock as a BUY.
